title: Practical RAG Systems
objective: Set the deck frame and audience.
coreClaim: RAG is a production evidence-delivery architecture, not a vector database demo.
practicalTakeaway: Evaluate RAG by whether it delivers trusted evidence into a decision workflow.

title: Learning Goals
objective: Define what learners should be able to do after the deck.
coreClaim: Product and solutions teams need to reason about retrieval flow, data ownership, database fit, evaluation, and rollout risk.
practicalTakeaway: Use the deck as an architecture and discovery checklist.

title: The Practical Promise
coreClaim: The value of RAG is governed answers over proprietary, changing, permissioned knowledge.
practicalTakeaway: Sell outcomes such as answer quality, speed, citation, and governance, not embeddings.

title: What RAG Owns
objective: Separate RAG from model-only and search-only thinking.
coreClaim: RAG coordinates retrieval, evidence packing, generation, citation, and feedback around a user request.
practicalTakeaway: Draw the system boundary before choosing tools.

title: Reference Architecture
coreClaim: A production RAG architecture has separate ingestion, index, query, answer, and evaluation loops.
practicalTakeaway: Missing loops become production failure modes.

title: Two Use Cases
coreClaim: Support escalation and proposal/compliance workflows expose the practical constraints generic demos hide.
practicalTakeaway: Use cases should stress permissions, freshness, exact tokens, citations, and workflow handoff.

title: Support Copilot Problem
coreClaim: Support RAG must combine symptoms, logs, versions, incidents, tickets, customer context, and docs.
practicalTakeaway: If the system cannot see customer and version context, it will overgeneralize.

title: Support Evidence Model
objective: Map source data to retrieval objects.
coreClaim: Support evidence needs source lineage, tenant visibility, product/version metadata, and operational semantics.
practicalTakeaway: Metadata is a retrieval feature and an access-control feature.

title: Support Ingestion Flow
objective: Show how source systems become searchable evidence.
coreClaim: RAG ingestion should preserve raw source, parsed text, chunk records, embeddings, index status, and source version.
practicalTakeaway: Never make the vector index the only source ledger.

title: Support Chunking
objective: Explain chunk design for operational content.
coreClaim: Chunks should align to problem-solving units such as symptom, cause, workaround, fix version, command, and caveat.
practicalTakeaway: Chunking quality decides whether retrieval gives the model useful evidence.

title: Support Query Planning
objective: Show how user questions become retrieval plans.
coreClaim: Support questions need entity extraction, filters, query rewriting, and exact-token preservation before search.
practicalTakeaway: Do not embed away error codes, SKUs, versions, and tenant identifiers.

title: Support Retrieval
coreClaim: Support RAG usually needs lexical, vector, structured filters, and reranking in one flow.
practicalTakeaway: Use vector search for meaning and lexical search for exact operational clues.

title: Support Answer Contract
coreClaim: The answer should include cited diagnosis, next checks, likely fix, confidence, and escalation triggers.
practicalTakeaway: An answer contract makes quality observable and reviewable.

title: Support Ops Loop
objective: Connect runtime failures back to content and retrieval fixes.
coreClaim: Misses, stale evidence, conflicts, and user feedback should become source and eval backlog items.
practicalTakeaway: Production RAG improves through measured loops, not prompt tweaks alone.

title: Support Architecture Decision
objective: Summarize platform fit for the support use case.
coreClaim: The right database depends on source ownership, filters, update path, joins, and vector-serving needs.
practicalTakeaway: Choose the store that owns the hardest operational boundary.

title: Proposal Engine Problem
coreClaim: Proposal and compliance RAG is governed answer reuse over approved, sensitive, and versioned evidence.
practicalTakeaway: Correctness includes approval state and disclosure rules, not just semantic relevance.

title: Authority Model
objective: Show source authority and approval states.
coreClaim: Proposal RAG needs source authority, ownership, effective dates, disclosure level, and review state.
practicalTakeaway: The system must know which evidence can be used automatically.

title: Proposal Ingestion
objective: Map controlled documents to retrieval units.
coreClaim: Evidence units should map to clauses, controls, approved answers, policy sections, and exception notes.
practicalTakeaway: Model source governance before embedding the corpus.

title: Question Classification
objective: Explain query routing for proposal work.
coreClaim: Incoming questions should route differently for factual answers, controls, clauses, roadmap claims, and human review.
practicalTakeaway: A single retrieval strategy is too blunt for liability-sensitive workflows.

title: Governed Retrieval
objective: Show retrieval with approval and disclosure constraints.
coreClaim: Filters for approval state, disclosure level, customer segment, region, and source owner are core product logic.
practicalTakeaway: Governed retrieval should prevent the wrong evidence from entering the prompt.

title: Review Workflow
coreClaim: Proposal RAG should produce draft answers with citations, confidence, and review routing rather than unconditional auto-send.
practicalTakeaway: Human approval is part of the product when liability is high.

title: Answer Lifecycle
objective: Close the loop from approved answer to reusable evidence.
coreClaim: Approved answers should flow back into the retrieval corpus with ownership, version, and expiry metadata.
practicalTakeaway: A RAG product creates governed knowledge assets as users work.

title: Retrieval Pattern Map
objective: Build the taxonomy of retrieval patterns.
coreClaim: Production RAG uses dense, lexical, structured, hybrid, graph, and reranked retrieval depending on question shape.
practicalTakeaway: Match retrieval pattern to user intent.

title: Dense, Lexical, Structured
coreClaim: Dense search captures meaning, lexical search captures tokens, and structured queries enforce exact business constraints.
practicalTakeaway: Hybrid systems exist because real questions mix signals.

title: Hybrid Search
objective: Explain rank fusion and score handling.
coreClaim: Hybrid search combines multiple candidate lists; score normalization and rank fusion are implementation choices.
practicalTakeaway: Treat scores as store-local ranking signals, not universal truth.

title: Filters and Permissions
coreClaim: Tenant, role, region, date, product, version, and approval filters should constrain retrieval as early as possible.
practicalTakeaway: Post-filtering after retrieval can leak evidence and distort recall.

title: Reranking
coreClaim: Rerankers improve ordering by scoring query-document pairs after broad candidate retrieval.
practicalTakeaway: Add reranking when recall is good but the top answer set is noisy.

title: Context Packing
objective: Show how retrieved evidence becomes prompt context.
coreClaim: Context packing must balance relevance, diversity, authority, recency, citation coverage, and token budget.
practicalTakeaway: The LLM should receive compact evidence, not a heap of chunks.

title: Failure Modes
coreClaim: RAG failures can originate in source policy, parsing, chunking, embedding, filters, retrieval, reranking, prompting, or UX.
practicalTakeaway: Debug the pipeline stage that actually failed.

title: Database Responsibilities
coreClaim: Databases in RAG may own source truth, chunk metadata, vectors, lexical indexes, workflow state, traces, and eval datasets.
practicalTakeaway: A vector index is only one database responsibility.

title: MongoDB Angle
coreClaim: MongoDB is strongest when RAG evidence, metadata, workflow state, and product records are document-shaped and operationally close.
practicalTakeaway: Use MongoDB when retrieval should live beside document data and application workflow.

title: pgvector Angle
objective: Describe where Postgres plus pgvector fits.
coreClaim: pgvector is strongest when vector search extends an existing Postgres application with SQL, joins, ACID, and governance.
practicalTakeaway: Use pgvector when Postgres is already the durable product boundary.

title: Vector-First Systems
objective: Describe Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, and Milvus fit.
coreClaim: Vector-first systems are strongest when retrieval serving deserves its own specialized tier.
practicalTakeaway: Pair vector-first stores with a clear source-of-truth and governance model.

title: Search Engines
objective: Describe OpenSearch, Elasticsearch, and Azure AI Search fit.
coreClaim: Search-engine platforms are strong when lexical relevance, analyzers, semantic ranking, and enterprise search operations dominate.
practicalTakeaway: Choose search-engine lineage when enterprise search is the core competency.

title: Chroma and Local Stores
coreClaim: Local vector stores are excellent for prototyping and smaller self-hosted workflows but do not remove production governance needs.
practicalTakeaway: Prototype locally, then decide which production boundary the database must own.

title: Comparison Matrix
coreClaim: The useful comparison dimensions are source ownership, filters, hybrid retrieval, update freshness, operations, and workflow fit.
practicalTakeaway: Start with workload constraints, then benchmark.

title: Architecture Choices
objective: Turn comparison into a decision framework.
coreClaim: Use one-store, app-db-plus-search, or vector-tier architecture based on ownership and operational boundaries.
practicalTakeaway: Do not split data planes unless the split solves a real operational problem.

title: Evaluation Pyramid
coreClaim: Evaluation should cover retrieval, context, answer, workflow outcome, and operational health.
practicalTakeaway: A single answer-quality score is not enough.

title: Metrics That Matter
coreClaim: RAG teams need offline retrieval metrics, answer faithfulness metrics, latency/cost metrics, and user outcome metrics.
practicalTakeaway: Tie metrics to product decisions and release gates.

title: Observability
coreClaim: Useful RAG observability captures query, filters, candidates, scores, rerank, context, answer, citations, latency, cost, and feedback.
practicalTakeaway: If you cannot inspect a bad answer, you cannot improve it reliably.

title: Rollout Plan
coreClaim: Production RAG should roll out through scoped corpus, gold set, offline benchmark, human-in-the-loop pilot, and monitored expansion.
practicalTakeaway: Ship trust gradually with evidence and controls.

title: Recap
coreClaim: Practical RAG is a governed retrieval product with a database architecture decision inside it.
practicalTakeaway: Design around source truth, retrieval quality, permissions, evaluation, and operational ownership.

title: References
coreClaim: Primary sources should be grouped by concept for follow-up reading.
practicalTakeaway: Use current docs and workload evidence before making platform claims.
